locked_facts: All angles measured from NORMAL, never from surface
Ray bends TOWARD normal entering denser medium
Ray bends AWAY from normal entering rarer medium
Speed of light in medium: v = c/n
truth_statements: When a ray of light passes from one medium to another, it changes direction at the interface
The ratio of sine of angle of incidence to sine of angle of refraction is constant for a given pair of media
A ray going from rarer to denser medium bends toward the normal
The refractive index of a medium equals the ratio of speed of light in vacuum to speed in the medium
Angles of incidence and refraction are always measured from the normal

aha_moment.what_to_show: Show incident ray going from air (n=1) to glass (n=1.5). The ray bends TOWARD the normal. Now reverse: glass to air — the ray bends AWAY from the normal. The direction of bending is entirely determined by which medium is denser. n₁ sinθ₁ = n₂ sinθ₂ predicts the exact angle every time.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Draw the normal prominently. Show angle arcs with degree values. Highlight that angles are from the normal, NOT from the surface.

simulation_states.label: Incident ray hits the interface
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Two media with different shading. Incident ray approaching the flat interface. Normal drawn as dotted line.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: The normal is perpendicular to the surface at the point of incidence. All angles are measured from this normal.

simulation_states.label: Rarer to denser — bends toward normal
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Refracted ray shown bending toward the normal. Angle arcs displayed. θ₂ < θ₁.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: Going from rarer (air) to denser (glass): the ray slows down and bends toward the normal. sinθ₂ < sinθ₁.

simulation_states.label: Snell's Law verified — n₁ sinθ₁ = n₂ sinθ₂
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Both angles displayed with their sine values. n₁ sinθ₁ and n₂ sinθ₂ shown to be equal.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: The product n·sinθ stays constant across the interface. This is Snell's Law.

simulation_states.label: Interactive — change the angle
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Slider to change angle of incidence. Refracted ray angle updates in real time via Snell's law.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: As you increase the angle of incidence, the refracted angle increases too — but always less (for denser medium).

trigger_keywords: glass mein jaane pe normal se door
common_misconceptions.student_belief: A ray bends away from the normal when entering a denser medium
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: A ray bends TOWARD the normal when entering a denser medium (higher n). It bends AWAY from the normal when entering a rarer medium (lower n). The ray slows down in a denser medium and the component along the interface is preserved by Snell's law.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show a clear animation: ray going from air to glass bends TOWARD normal, ray going from glass to air bends AWAY from normal. Mark the normal prominently so the direction is unambiguous.
